Kind regards Employee 21 Notes: 26.11.24 Ticket opened by Employee 16. 26.11.24 Ticket assigned SOURCE 10: ﻿Blueprint: Identity & Authentication (AD FS / Entra ID Integration) Version / History Version | Date | Author | Notes 0.1 | 11.02.2026 | PS | Creation (Initial) 0.2 | – | – | – General Information and Description This is the blueprint for Identity & Authentication (AD FS / Entra ID Integration). Active Directory Federation Services (AD FS) is an identity service provided by Microsoft that enables organizations to centrally manage authentication and authorization processes and to provide Single Sign-On (SSO) for both internal and external applications. AD FS is commonly used in hybrid IT environments where on-premises applications, cloud services, and external partners are integrated. In combination with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory), AD FS enables the issuance of security tokens (e.g., SAML or JWT/OpenID Connect), which are used by target applications for authentication and authorization. Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) mechanisms can optionally be integrated, either directly via AD FS or through connected claims providers. A key characteristic of this architecture is that different authentication flows may exist: • Browser-based logins (e.g., web interfaces) • Non-browser-based logins (e.g., services, integrations, APIs) As a result, it is possible for a login via browser to succeed while an application or integration fails to connect despite correct credentials. In such cases, the root cause is often inconsistent client parameters, token format incompatibilities, or implicit MFA delegations that are not immediately visible during standard troubleshooting. This blueprint describes the fundamental architecture, the relevant design decisions, and typical integration scenarios for AD FS-based authentication. Licensing The following licensing principles apply to this architecture: • Microsoft Entra ID Free / P1 / P2 (depending on required features such as Conditional Access or MFA) • Windows Server license for operating AD FS • No additional cloud license is required specifically for AD FS The specific licensing model depends on the scope of authentication, the number of users, and security requirements (e.g., MFA, Conditional Access). Best Practices Our best practices are aligned with Microsoft’s official recommendations and include in particular: • Clear separation between browser-based and application-based authentication • Deliberate choice of SAML or JWT (OIDC) per application • Documentation of all client registrations and parameters • Careful use of MFA delegation to external claims providers • Regular review of certificates and token signatures Desing Decision tree & use cases Decision Tree – Authentication Issues AD FS / Entra ID / MFA 1. Question: Can users successfully sign in via a browser-based interface (e.g., web login)? • Yes: Proceed to question 2. • No: Check the AD FS service and the user account. Then reassess the ticket. 2. Question: Does the issue occur with an application or integration without a browser (e.g., service, API, background process)? • Yes: Proceed to question 3. • No: Check the application configuration outside of this blueprint. 3. Question: Do you know which authentication protocol the application uses? • OpenID Connect / OAuth2 (JWT): Proceed to question 4. • SAML: Proceed to question 6. • Unknown: Clarify the authentication mechanism with the application owner. 4. Question: Is the client correctly registered in AD FS (Client ID, Redirect URI)? • Yes: Proceed to question 5. • No: Correct the client registration and test again. 5. Question: Is the application designed to process JWT tokens? • Yes: Proceed to question 7. • No: Check whether a switch to SAML is required or whether the application must be adapted. 6. Question: Do other SAML-based applications work with the same AD FS configuration? • Yes: Check the specific SAML configuration of the affected application. • No: Check the general SAML configuration in AD FS. 7. Question: Is Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) enabled in this sign-in process? • Yes: Proceed to question 8. • No: Proceed to question 9. 8. Question: Is MFA requested via an external claims provider? • Yes: Check whether the application supports MFA. • No: Check the MFA configuration directly in AD FS. 9. Question: Does the error occur only sporadically or only for specific users? • Yes: Check certificates, time deviations, and existing sessions. • No: Proceed to question 10. 10. Question: Was the issue resolved by adjusting AD FS parameters or client settings? • Yes: Document the change and close the ticket. • No: Escalate the case to the Identity or Architecture team. Use Cases Use Case 1 – Login works in the browser but not in the application A user can sign in via the web interface, but a connected application or integration fails. The decision tree helps to isolate the difference between browser-based and application-based authentication. Use Case 2 – Authentication error after configuration change After adjustments to AD FS or client settings, new sign-in errors occur. The decision tree supports systematically reviewing the relevant parameters. Use Case 3 – Intermittent or user-specific errors Sign-in works only intermittently or only for certain users. The decision tree guides targeted checks of certificates, time deviations, and session data. Use Case 4 – Escalation decision If no clear root cause can be identified, the decision tree helps determine when escalation is required and when further analysis is appropriate. REPORT 28: # Train/Toolbox ADFS MFA Integration (New ADFS Client) — Production Authentication Failures The community centers on an ADFS-based authentication integration for the Train system where the Toolbox client relied on a newly registered ADFS client to perform MFA requests. The new ADFS client registration contained two parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) set in a way that changed token format (JWT issuance) and delegated MFA to external claims providers; these changes broke expected authentication flows and produced reproducible failures across environments. Troubleshooting initially focused on environment changes in PROD, but investigation identified the misconfigured ADFS client registration as the root cause; remediation consisted of aligning the registration parameters with TEST and updating federation data and certificates. The incident has operational, diagnostic, and governance implications: token-format compatibility, MFA delegation behavior, increased logging/auditing complexity, and the need for tighter registration/configuration controls and runbooks for ADFS client lifecycle and MFA expectation management. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,237,95); Relationships (390,370,65)] ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration for the new Toolbox MFA client The authentication failures were traced to a newly introduced client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set in a way that changed the authentication flow and produced unexpected token handling and MFA delegation. The investigation documents the new client, its registration object, and the problematic parameter settings as the primary configuration unit responsible for the failures. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,241,240); Relationships (390,370)] Remediation applied during the incident included adjusting the ADFS client registration parameters to match the TEST environment, updating federation data, removing old certificates, and verifying ADFS configuration — actions that directly addressed the misconfiguration and resolved the observed failures. This establishes the registration entry as the high-value control point for preventing recurrence. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## Toolbox/Train MFA flows depended on the new client and reproduced failures across environments The Toolbox client on the Train system used the new ADFS-registered client specifically to perform MFA requests; since the client registration changed both token type and MFA handling, Toolbox sign-in flows were disrupted. The documentation explicitly links Toolbox to the new client and describes how those client settings led to the observed authentication issues. [Data: Entities (237,249,95); Relationships (370,372)] Investigators initially assessed ADFS and considered excluding it because only PROD had been modified over the weekend, and errors reproduced across environments; however, further analysis found the registration mismatch as the root cause, underscoring the importance of configuration parity and the traceability of client registrations across environments. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65,370)] ## EnableJWT introduces token-format compatibility risk (ADFS -> JWT vs SAML expectations) EnableJWT in the ADFS client registration causes ADFS to issue JSON Web Tokens (JWTs); when downstream applications expect SAML or another token format, they may be unable to validate or consume JWTs, causing authentication failures. The records describe EnableJWT and JWT as directly linked and note that enabling JWT issuance can break downstream systems not designed to accept JWTs. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379,416,335)] Because the new client had EnableJWT enabled, this change in token type is a concrete compatibility vector that must be validated for each relying application before enabling in production; the blueprint notes deliberate selection of token format per application is required. Any rollback or permanent change should ensure token-format compatibility is validated in staging and documented in the client registration. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379)] ## RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA and increases operational/logging complexity The RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders parameter, when enabled, forwards MFA requirements to configured external claims providers, effectively delegating execution of MFA to those providers. That delegation alters the authentication flow and changes where and how MFA is performed, impacting traceability of MFA events. The records describe this parameter's behavior and its direct effect on MFA handling. [Data: Entities (241,95); Relationships (382,417)] Forwarding MFA to external claims providers also increases complexity for logging, monitoring, and audit trails because the system must reconcile MFA outcomes produced by external providers with internal access control logic. The analysis explicitly calls out logging and auditing implications tied to this parameter, which complicates diagnostics and compliance reporting unless telemetry and correlation are improved. [Data: Entities (243,241); Relationships (383)] ## Operational/process gap: PROD-only changes and environment parity hindered quicker identification Investigators initially focused on changes in PROD because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproducing across environments led to ADFS being initially considered but then effectively excluded at first. This indicates an operational blind spot: PROD-only changes can create cognitive bias when troubleshooting and reveal weaknesses in change-control or environment parity practices. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65)] The eventual identification of the misconfigured client registration demonstrates that configuration drift (or undocumented registration differences) between environments can mask root causes and delay remediation. Tighter deployment controls, registration promotion processes, and automated configuration comparisons between environments would reduce time-to-resolution. [Data: Entities (248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## ADFS client registration is the primary governance control point and needs stronger lifecycle and validation controls The ADFS client registration object contains the parameters that directly control token format and MFA delegation; the incident shows that a single registration entry can change authentication semantics across an integration. The records treat the client registration as the configuration unit used to represent the new client in ADFS and as the locus for remediation. [Data: Entities (248,249,21); Relationships (390)] This implies governance actions: versioned registration templates, pre-acceptance checks for EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders, mandatory compatibility tests per relying party (SAML vs JWT), and an approval workflow for registration changes. These controls will reduce the chance that a single mis-set parameter can cause production authentication failures. [Data: Entities (248,21); Relationships (390,379,382)] ## Monitoring, auditing, and runbooks must be updated to handle MFA delegation and token-format changes Because enabling RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA to external providers and enabling EnableJWT changes token format, logging and audit pipelines must be able to correlate external MFA claims and JWT issuance events back to internal sessions and access decisions. The analysis explicitly flags logging and auditing implications when MFA is forwarded to claims providers. [Data: Entities (241,243,242); Relationships (383,416)] Actionable operational changes include: adding telemetry to record when MFA is delegated, instrumenting JWT issuance events, updating SIEM parsing rules to recognize external MFA claim types and JWT indicators, and creating an incident runbook that includes steps for validating client registration parameters, token-format checks, and claims-provider health checks. These measures are required to maintain diagnostic clarity and compliance posture. [Data: Entities (243,241,240); Relationships (383,379)] ## Priority remediation and governance recommendations (actionable and ordered) Immediate actions: revert or align the problematic ADFS client registration parameters in PROD to the known-good TEST values (which was performed during the incident), validate federation data and certificate status, and confirm Toolbox/Train sign-in behavior in a controlled environment before re-enabling changes. The record of remediation includes changing the ADFS client parameters and updating federation data and certificates. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] Short-to-medium term governance: implement mandatory registration review and approval workflows, require token-format compatibility testing (SAML vs JWT) for each relying party, require explicit validation when enabling RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders including test coverage for external claims-provider responses, add SIEM/monitoring rules to surface unexpected token types and delegated MFA events, and codify an ADFS client registration checklist and runbook for escalations. These steps directly address the identified technical faults and operational weaknesses surfaced by the incident. [Data: Entities (248,241,240,243); Relationships (379,382,383)]

In some cases, access works for certain users after logging out and back in, but not for others. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 31: # ADFS Client Registration, Finance Conditional Access, and Hybrid Device Sync (Employee 21-led Remediation) This community centers on a set of identity and access failures affecting finance resources that were investigated and remediated by Employee 21. The immediate technical issues included misconfigured ADFS client registration parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) and delayed device state synchronization for hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices, which caused Conditional Access (Require compliant device for Finance Resources) to evaluate as not satisfied and block access for some users. Employee 21 authored consolidated ADFS configuration documentation, triggered manual device synchronization during remediation, and adjusted a Conditional Access rule (excluding a SharePoint project folder) to restore access; the same engineer also conducted WAF analysis that identified TLS inspection causing 502 errors and helped apply configuration adjustments that restored external access. The primary entities and relationships are: Employee 21 as the operational owner and author of the ADFS documentation, the ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION itself, the Finance Conditional Access policy, Hybrid Azure AD joined devices and the DEVICE SYNCHRONIZATION process, DEVICE COMPLIANCE STATUS results in Entra ID, and PRIVATE DEVICES which produced additional variability in access outcomes. [Data: Entities (232, 244, 680, 683, 684, 688, 685), Relationships (364, 1189, 1199, 1201, 1206, 1208, 365)] ## Single operational lead (Employee 21) conducted analysis, remediation, and documentation. Employee 21 served as the assigned ticket owner and lead analyst across multiple related incidents, performed remote troubleshooting, coordinated remediation with the Network Team, adjusted Conditional Access, triggered device synchronization, and authored the ADFS configuration notes and root‑cause analysis that identified misconfigured ADFS client registration parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) [Data: Entities (232), Relationships (1189, 365, 364)]. Having a single visible owner accelerated the investigation and produced formal documentation (ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION) and tactical remediations; this centralization is beneficial for rapid response but creates a single person dependency for institutional knowledge unless the documentation and handoff processes are formalized. The existence of authored documentation suggests an opportunity to convert incident knowledge into maintained runbooks and to ensure alternate owners are trained and assigned. ## Root cause for token/MFA failures: misconfigured ADFS client registration parameters. The documented root cause for the JWT/MFA login failures was ADFS client registration parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) being misconfigured; Employee 21 authored notes describing the corrected settings and consolidated those into a formal ADFS configuration document intended to align production to TEST‑environment values [Data: Entities (232, 244), Relationships (364)]. This indicates the authentication failure was caused by identity provider configuration drift rather than client or network connectivity alone. The formalization into ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION provides a basis for preventing recurrence if it is enforced via configuration management, automated checks, or periodic reviews against the documented expected values. ## Conditional Access policy interaction with device compliance blocked access to finance resources. The tenant Conditional Access policy titled 'Require compliant device for Finance Resources' evaluated device compliance as a condition; where device compliance status was 'unknown' or stale the policy evaluated as 'not satisfied', preventing access until compliance updated. The policy was adjusted (a SharePoint project folder excluded) as part of remediation to restore access for impacted users [Data: Entities (680, 232), Relationships (1201, 1199)]. This demonstrates a multi‑layered failure mode: identity provider configuration problems coincident with device compliance evaluation delays led to user impact for business‑critical (Finance) resources. The temporary exclusion of a resource from the CA policy is an appropriate tactical mitigation but should be followed by root cause fixes (device sync reliability and ADFS configuration) and a formal risk assessment to determine whether broader policy tuning or exceptions are warranted. ## Hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices experienced delayed/faulty synchronization, causing 'unknown' compliance and access failures. Hybrid Azure AD joined devices experienced faulty or delayed synchronization to Entra ID (6–8 hour lag), leaving device compliance status reported as 'unknown' and delaying Conditional Access evaluation and access restoration for some users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683, 684, 688), Relationships (1206, 1189)]. Delayed device synchronization is a systemic reliability issue for hybrid device management that can repeatedly cause Conditional Access denials for otherwise compliant endpoints. Manual syncs are effective as an incident workaround but indicate the need for monitoring, alerting, and automation around device sync health, plus investigation into root causes (connectivity, AD‑connector health, sync throttling, or service limits) and capacity for remediation. ## Private/personal devices contributed to variability in access outcomes due to non‑managed compliance states. Some users accessed resources from private devices which are more likely to report non‑compliant or 'unknown' device status in Entra ID; this was observed in the incident and contributed to Conditional Access evaluation failures [Data: Entities (685, 688), Relationships (1208)]. Private devices introduce expected variability when CA policies require device compliance. The organization should treat private devices as higher‑risk by design (e.g., restrict finance resource access, require stronger controls, or use just‑in‑time access workflows) and ensure user education and policy clarity to reduce helpdesk load from predictable denial scenarios. ## WAF/TLS inspection caused 502 errors for external access; configuration adjustments restored service. During later work Employee 21 performed WAF analysis and identified TLS inspection as the cause of 502 errors when enabled; they compared the WAF configuration to the internal blueprint and helped apply configuration adjustments that restored external access [Data: Entities (232)]. This is an example of infrastructure security tooling (TLS inspection) interacting poorly with application traffic, producing availability issues. The finding underscores the need to validate WAF/inspection rules against blueprints and application behavior, include regression tests for TLS inspection changes, and maintain a rollback plan for inspection rules that disrupt production traffic. ## Documentation exists but should be operationalized into governance, monitoring, and runbooks. A consolidated ADFS CONFIGURATION DOCUMENTATION was recorded to capture the corrected ADFS client registration and investigation details; Employee 21 confirmed the configuration and notes have been documented to avoid future problems [Data: Entities (244), Relationships (364)]. Documentation is a necessary first step; to reduce operational risk this content should be converted into enforceable controls: baseline configuration templates, configuration drift detection (CI/CD or configuration management), automated compliance checks, runbooks for manual and automated remediation (including device sync triggers), and clearly mapped escalation/owner lists so incidents do not rely on a single individual's presence.

[Data: Entities (248,241,240,243); Relationships (379,382,383)] REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 8: # SharePoint folder access — Sensitivity Label vs ACL (Customer Company 25 AG / Compliance_Review_2025) This community centers on a recurring SharePoint access problem in Customer Company 25 AG where a folder (/Projects/Compliance_Review_2025) was granted unique SharePoint ACLs, then later had a Microsoft Information Protection sensitivity label applied. The label enforced encryption tied to a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) while the SharePoint ACL referenced SP_Compliance_Main (which in turn referenced a cloud-created COMPLIANCE_TEAM). Azure AD Connect synchronization of an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM object with the same display name (different ObjectID) replaced or changed the cloud object evaluation, excluding cloud-only external guests and producing access loss. Token caching and token-claim timing caused the symptom to appear intermittent (users could temporarily access with stale tokens, then lose access when tokens refreshed). The issue was reproduced in a TEST TENANT, investigated using Azure AD sign-in logs and Microsoft Purview, escalated through HELPDESK → BU Infrastructure (M365/Identity) → System Engineers, remediated by group consolidation / full sync / reapply label / invalidate tokens, and validated by the customer's Audit Team and Consulting Sample Company. ## Primary technical root cause: label encryption permissions diverged from SharePoint ACLs because the label was applied after unique permissions and 'Preserve custom permissions' was not enabled The folder /Projects/Compliance_Review_2025 had Unique Permissions (a SharePoint ACL) applied, and the Sensitivity Label ("Confidential – Internal") was applied later; because the label was applied without enabling the "Preserve custom permissions" option, the label's encryption referenced a different Azure AD group (Compliance_Core) than the ACL did, producing a permissions divergence that prevented decryption for some users. This sequence (ACL → label) and the missing preserve option are explicitly documented in the incident analysis and relationships examined during the investigation. This misalignment meant that SharePoint could show an ACL granting access (SP_Compliance_Main) while the label-based encryption check denied decryption because the token claims did not show membership in Compliance_Core. The label-enforced encryption thus became the effective gatekeeper for content access in practice, overriding the expectation set by the ACL alone. The incident record links the folder, the label configuration and the preserve-permissions option directly to the observed inconsistent access behavior. Azure AD Connect synchronization behavior caused the synchronized On‑Prem group object to overwrite or replace the cloud object reference used by SharePoint, which excluded cloud-only guest members and caused external guests to lose access when SharePoint evaluated the synchronized group object. System Engineer 14 and the identity team reproduced and explained this behaviour using Microsoft Graph queries and by reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior. [Data: Entities (269, 265, 266, 267); Relationships (446, 437, 430)] ## Token caching and claim timing produced intermittent/partial access symptoms Multiple records indicate that cached or previously-issued authentication tokens contained older group claims (e.g., COMPLIANCE_TEAM) that temporarily allowed access until tokens were refreshed or re-evaluated — at which point the absence of the Compliance_Core claim caused decryption to fail. Browser session state (including Incognito vs normal sessions) and Office client sessions retained tokens that influenced who saw the folder and when. Azure AD sign-in logs and token/claim records showed affected tokens contained COMPLIANCE_TEAM claims but lacked Compliance_Core; sign-in log timing and Conditional Access evaluation events correlated with observed intermittent behavior. The use of a non-production test tenant allowed safe reproduction of the sequence and validation of remediation steps before applying them to production, and test findings (including incognito behavior) were used to justify the combined remediation approach (group consolidation + full sync + token invalidation + label reapplication). The Customer Company 25 AG incidents specifically documented external project members losing access to project folders and required escalation to BU Infrastructure – M365/Identity and system engineers for in‑depth remediation. The recurrence and the number of escalations indicate a systemic interaction between label application processes, group/object management and synchronization rules that goes beyond a single, transient misconfiguration — and therefore warrants process and architecture changes to prevent repeat outages. Communication paths (helpdesk ↔ contact persons, identity BU ↔ system engineers, consulting vendor ↔ customer) were used effectively to resolve the incidents. However, the root cause — interplay between SharePoint ACLs, sensitivity label configuration, and Azure AD group/object management — crosses service, identity and compliance boundaries. This cross-domain ownership means that explicit, codified governance is required for label application (who may apply labels and how), group naming/object‑ID collision handling, and Azure AD Connect change control in order to avoid reintroducing the same fault through routine administrative changes. [Data: Entities (85, 549, 261, 548, 568, 572); Relationships (297, 944, 967, 971)] ## Security/compliance configuration nuance: labels enforced encryption but ACL/label misalignment can deny intended principals Sensitivity Labels managed through Microsoft Purview/AIP enforce encryption that is evaluated against Azure AD group membership (e.g., requiring membership in Compliance_Core). The label mechanism is therefore capable of blocking decryption even when SharePoint ACLs indicate permission. In this incident, applying the label without preserving custom ACL mapping effectively changed the authority model for access, producing potential availability impacts for legitimate users and creating a risk that compliance-related labels could be misapplied in ways that disrupt business processes. The investigation used Purview tooling to review label configuration and concluded that label application options (such as "Preserve custom permissions") materially affect access outcomes; thus label application must be treated as both a security and availability control, and require pre‑application checks and a rollback/test path. [Data: Entities (557, 559, 556); Relationships (977, 959, 981)] ## Prioritized, actionable recommendations to prevent recurrence Based on the documented causes and remediation steps, the following prioritized actions are indicated: (1) Treat label application as a controlled change — enable "Preserve custom permissions" or explicitly map ACL → label group mappings and test in a TEST TENANT prior to production; (2) Implement Azure AD Connect governance: detect display-name collisions, require ObjectID reconciliation/explicit mapping for groups with identical display names, and use pre-sync validation to prevent cloud group overwrite; (3) Add a runbook to force token revocation and clear Office/browser sessions after any group or label change (sign‑out guidance, token revocation, and customer communication); (4) Maintain a pre-label checklist (verify group referenced by label exists and contains intended principals including guests) and require Purview review/approval for applied encryption labels; (5) Add monitoring/alerts for rapid detection: track label application events, group-object replacements, and anomalous sign-in errors referencing encryption failures. REPORT 1: # NDES / Intune NDES Connector and AD CS (Issuing CA) Community This community centers on the on‑premises NDES service and its integration point with Microsoft Intune via the Intune NDES Connector, backed by Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) and Issuing CA/template configuration. The authoritative blueprint (NDES/certificates/Intune) documents the decision tree, required IIS components, service account permissions, and monitoring recommendations used to install, validate, and escalate NDES issues. Recent operational evidence shows the Intune NDES Connector lost communication, was reinstalled without proxy settings, and returned to a healthy state in Azure after firewall and reinstall actions; logs and a referenced blueprint bundle were used to validate the remediation. Key high‑risk areas are certificate template/CA misconfiguration, IIS component and service‑account permissions, and cloud-to-on‑prem connectivity (proxy/firewall) for the connector. [Data: Entities (360,641,361,359); Relationships (584,1110,579,1109)] ## NDES depends on AD CS (Issuing CA) and correct AD CS post‑configuration to issue certificates NDES is not a standalone issuer — it relies on AD CS (Issuing CA) and correctly configured certificate templates and AD CS post‑configuration to perform certificate issuance. The blueprint and relationship mapping explicitly state that NDES depends on AD CS and that AD CS post‑configuration must be completed before NDES will issue certificates [Data: Entities (360,361,369); Relationships (584,592)]. When AD CS templates, Issuing CA selection, or post‑configuration steps are incorrect or incomplete, certificate issuance will fail and manifest as authentication failures or inability to enroll devices. The blueprint and CA/template relationships call out misconfigured templates and incorrect CA selection as common failure modes that require CA‑side remediation [Data: Entities (365,364,361); Relationships (602,589)]. ## Intune NDES Connector outage traced to proxy/firewall and resolved by reinstall + firewall changes Operational records indicate the Intune NDES Connector lost communication with Intune, was reinstalled without proxy configuration, and reported a healthy ("green") status in Azure following remediation steps; the connector is used to bridge Intune cloud SCEP flows to on‑prem NDES [Data: Entities (641,642,650); Relationships (1110,1113,1109)]. Firewall rule adjustments were part of the remediation that allowed the connector to re‑establish communication. Logs were attached to the ticket/email to validate connector behavior after reinstallation, demonstrating the diagnostic steps taken to confirm recovery [Data: Entities (649); Relationships (1108,1112)]. ## Missing IIS components and incorrect NDES service account permissions are frequent root causes The blueprint’s decision tree specifically checks for required IIS components (ASP.NET 3.5/4.7.2, Request Filtering, IIS6 Compatibility) and for correct permissioning of the NDES service account (membership in IIS_IUSRS or equivalent) as primary preconditions for NDES to start and operate [Data: Entities (367,373,366,368); Relationships (604,606,603)]. Absent IIS features or a misconfigured service account will prevent the NDES service from starting or responding even if AD CS and network connectivity are correct — these are documented, repeatable failure modes in the blueprint used for installation and troubleshooting guidance [Data: Entities (359,367); Relationships (579,604)]. ## Service account membership in IIS_IUSRS and host permissions are a documented single point of failure The blueprint requires the NDES service account be configured with appropriate host permissions (example: membership in IIS_IUSRS) to run the IIS‑hosted NDES endpoints. Relationship mappings explicitly tie the NDES service account to IIS_IUSRS and the IIS host, identifying this configuration as necessary for runtime permissions [Data: Entities (366,368,373); Relationships (603,606)]. Operationally, incorrect service‑account configuration is actionable and should be among the first items checked during NDES service start failures because it can be validated quickly and remediated without immediate AD CS changes, reducing mean time to repair when connectivity/CA issues are not present [Data: Entities (372,359); Relationships (607,579)]. ## Proxy and firewall configuration are critical connectivity controls for the Intune Connector and represent fragile cloud-to-on‑prem bridging The Intune NDES Connector functions as the cloud bridge for SCEP flows; changes to proxy configuration or firewall rules can directly interrupt connector communication, as shown when the connector was reinstalled without proxy settings and later required firewall adjustments to regain connectivity [Data: Entities (641,644,642); Relationships (1109,1108,1110)]. Because the connector is a required component for Intune SCEP profile delivery, network path and proxy/firewall controls are de facto operational dependencies — misconfiguration or transient network filtering can cause widespread device enrollment failures even when AD CS and NDES are healthy on‑prem [Data: Entities (641,651); Relationships (1113,1114)]. ## Monitoring and logs are in place per the blueprint but should be consolidated and tuned for certificate‑specific telemetry The blueprint includes explicit recommendations to monitor certificate requests, issuance errors, expiry, and communication failures between NDES, AD CS, and consuming devices; monitoring is cited as critical to detect indirect impacts like authentication failures or connectivity drops [Data: Entities (371,359); Relationships (583)]. Operational logs were attached to the incident response to validate connector behavior after remediation; however, the presence of logs alone is not sufficient — the community should ensure centralized alerting/thresholds for certificate request failures, template mismatches, CA expiry, and connector connectivity to reduce detection latency [Data: Entities (649,371); Relationships (1112,583,1122)]. ## Escalation and ownership are defined: decision tree directs to infrastructure/AD CS specialists for unresolved issuance problems The blueprint’s decision tree contains explicit escalation guidance: if the installation and NDES checks fail to resolve issuance problems, the next step is to escalate to infrastructure/AD CS specialists for deeper PKI, CA, and template remediation [Data: Entities (372,374,369); Relationships (607,600,592)]. This delineation clarifies operational handoffs — frontline technicians follow the decision tree and collect logs/evidence, then hand off to specialists for CA/template fixes or deeper server/PKI work, which reduces confusion over ownership during live incidents [Data: Entities (372,359); Relationships (607,579)]. ## Prioritized, actionable recommendations align with the blueprint: immediate fixes, medium‑term hardening, and governance Short‑term (Immediate): 1) Validate the Intune NDES Connector network path — confirm firewall rules and proxy settings are correct and documented; 2) Reinstall/validate connector with documented proxy configuration where required; 3) Use the attached logs to confirm successful SCEP enrollment tests to representative network devices and managed endpoints [Data: Entities (641,644,649,650); Relationships (1108,1109,1112,1113)]. Medium‑term (Weeks): 1) Implement a checklist from the blueprint’s decision tree that includes IIS component checks, service‑account membership in IIS_IUSRS, AD CS post‑configuration verification, and CA/template validation; 2) Centralize certificate issuance and connector telemetry (certificate request errors, CA expiries, connector health) into monitoring/alerting systems to detect regressions before user impact [Data: Entities (372,367,366,371); Relationships (579,603,583)]. Long‑term (Policy/Governance): 1) Formalize ownership and escalation runbooks that reference the blueprint and decision tree (frontline → infra/AD CS specialists) and ensure specialists have delegated procedures for CA/template changes; 2) Maintain a CA lifecycle and template review schedule to detect expiring root/issuing CA certs and misconfigured templates that would produce mass failures [Data: Entities (359,374,363,365); Relationships (607,596,602)].

context: SOURCE 10: ﻿Blueprint: Identity & Authentication (AD FS / Entra ID Integration) Version / History Version | Date | Author | Notes 0.1 | 11.02.2026 | PS | Creation (Initial) 0.2 | – | – | – General Information and Description This is the blueprint for Identity & Authentication (AD FS / Entra ID Integration). AD FS is commonly used in hybrid IT environments where on-premises applications, cloud services, and external partners are integrated. In combination with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory), AD FS enables the issuance of security tokens (e.g., SAML or JWT/OpenID Connect), which are used by target applications for authentication and authorization. Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) mechanisms can optionally be integrated, either directly via AD FS or through connected claims providers. A key characteristic of this architecture is that different authentication flows may exist: • Browser-based logins (e.g., web interfaces) • Non-browser-based logins (e.g., services, integrations, APIs) As a result, it is possible for a login via browser to succeed while an application or integration fails to connect despite correct credentials. In such cases, the root cause is often inconsistent client parameters, token format incompatibilities, or implicit MFA delegations that are not immediately visible during standard troubleshooting. This blueprint describes the fundamental architecture, the relevant design decisions, and typical integration scenarios for AD FS-based authentication. Licensing The following licensing principles apply to this architecture: • Microsoft Entra ID Free / P1 / P2 (depending on required features such as Conditional Access or MFA) • Windows Server license for operating AD FS • No additional cloud license is required specifically for AD FS The specific licensing model depends on the scope of authentication, the number of users, and security requirements (e.g., MFA, Conditional Access). Best Practices Our best practices are aligned with Microsoft’s official recommendations and include in particular: • Clear separation between browser-based and application-based authentication • Deliberate choice of SAML or JWT (OIDC) per application • Documentation of all client registrations and parameters • Careful use of MFA delegation to external claims providers • Regular review of certificates and token signatures Desing Decision tree & use cases Decision Tree – Authentication Issues AD FS / Entra ID / MFA 1. Question: Can users successfully sign in via a browser-based interface (e.g., web login)? • Yes: Proceed to question 2. • No: Check the AD FS service and the user account. Then reassess the ticket. 2. Question: Does the issue occur with an application or integration without a browser (e.g., service, API, background process)? • Yes: Proceed to question 3. • No: Check the application configuration outside of this blueprint. 3. Question: Do you know which authentication protocol the application uses? • OpenID Connect / OAuth2 (JWT): Proceed to question 4. • SAML: Proceed to question 6. • Unknown: Clarify the authentication mechanism with the application owner. 4. Question: Is the client correctly registered in AD FS (Client ID, Redirect URI)? • Yes: Proceed to question 5. • No: Correct the client registration and test again. 5. Question: Is the application designed to process JWT tokens? • Yes: Proceed to question 7. • No: Check whether a switch to SAML is required or whether the application must be adapted. 6. Question: Do other SAML-based applications work with the same AD FS configuration? • Yes: Check the specific SAML configuration of the affected application. • No: Check the general SAML configuration in AD FS. 7. Question: Is Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) enabled in this sign-in process? • Yes: Proceed to question 8. • No: Proceed to question 9. 8. Question: Is MFA requested via an external claims provider? • Yes: Check whether the application supports MFA. • No: Check the MFA configuration directly in AD FS. 9. Question: Does the error occur only sporadically or only for specific users? • Yes: Check certificates, time deviations, and existing sessions. • No: Proceed to question 10. 10. Question: Was the issue resolved by adjusting AD FS parameters or client settings? • Yes: Document the change and close the ticket. • No: Escalate the case to the Identity or Architecture team. Use Cases Use Case 1 – Login works in the browser but not in the application A user can sign in via the web interface, but a connected application or integration fails. The decision tree helps to isolate the difference between browser-based and application-based authentication. Use Case 2 – Authentication error after configuration change After adjustments to AD FS or client settings, new sign-in errors occur. The decision tree supports systematically reviewing the relevant parameters. Use Case 3 – Intermittent or user-specific errors Sign-in works only intermittently or only for certain users. The decision tree guides targeted checks of certificates, time deviations, and session data. Use Case 4 – Escalation decision If no clear root cause can be identified, the decision tree helps determine when escalation is required and when further analysis is appropriate. REPORT 28: # Train/Toolbox ADFS MFA Integration (New ADFS Client) — Production Authentication Failures The community centers on an ADFS-based authentication integration for the Train system where the Toolbox client relied on a newly registered ADFS client to perform MFA requests. The new ADFS client registration contained two parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) set in a way that changed token format (JWT issuance) and delegated MFA to external claims providers; these changes broke expected authentication flows and produced reproducible failures across environments. Troubleshooting initially focused on environment changes in PROD, but investigation identified the misconfigured ADFS client registration as the root cause; remediation consisted of aligning the registration parameters with TEST and updating federation data and certificates. The incident has operational, diagnostic, and governance implications: token-format compatibility, MFA delegation behavior, increased logging/auditing complexity, and the need for tighter registration/configuration controls and runbooks for ADFS client lifecycle and MFA expectation management. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,237,95); Relationships (390,370,65)] ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration for the new Toolbox MFA client The authentication failures were traced to a newly introduced client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set in a way that changed the authentication flow and produced unexpected token handling and MFA delegation. The investigation documents the new client, its registration object, and the problematic parameter settings as the primary configuration unit responsible for the failures. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,241,240); Relationships (390,370)] Remediation applied during the incident included adjusting the ADFS client registration parameters to match the TEST environment, updating federation data, removing old certificates, and verifying ADFS configuration — actions that directly addressed the misconfiguration and resolved the observed failures. This establishes the registration entry as the high-value control point for preventing recurrence. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## Toolbox/Train MFA flows depended on the new client and reproduced failures across environments The Toolbox client on the Train system used the new ADFS-registered client specifically to perform MFA requests; since the client registration changed both token type and MFA handling, Toolbox sign-in flows were disrupted. The documentation explicitly links Toolbox to the new client and describes how those client settings led to the observed authentication issues. [Data: Entities (237,249,95); Relationships (370,372)] Investigators initially assessed ADFS and considered excluding it because only PROD had been modified over the weekend, and errors reproduced across environments; however, further analysis found the registration mismatch as the root cause, underscoring the importance of configuration parity and the traceability of client registrations across environments. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65,370)] ## EnableJWT introduces token-format compatibility risk (ADFS -> JWT vs SAML expectations) EnableJWT in the ADFS client registration causes ADFS to issue JSON Web Tokens (JWTs); when downstream applications expect SAML or another token format, they may be unable to validate or consume JWTs, causing authentication failures. The records describe EnableJWT and JWT as directly linked and note that enabling JWT issuance can break downstream systems not designed to accept JWTs. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379,416,335)] Because the new client had EnableJWT enabled, this change in token type is a concrete compatibility vector that must be validated for each relying application before enabling in production; the blueprint notes deliberate selection of token format per application is required. Any rollback or permanent change should ensure token-format compatibility is validated in staging and documented in the client registration. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379)] ## RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA and increases operational/logging complexity The RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders parameter, when enabled, forwards MFA requirements to configured external claims providers, effectively delegating execution of MFA to those providers. That delegation alters the authentication flow and changes where and how MFA is performed, impacting traceability of MFA events. The records describe this parameter's behavior and its direct effect on MFA handling. [Data: Entities (241,95); Relationships (382,417)] Forwarding MFA to external claims providers also increases complexity for logging, monitoring, and audit trails because the system must reconcile MFA outcomes produced by external providers with internal access control logic. The analysis explicitly calls out logging and auditing implications tied to this parameter, which complicates diagnostics and compliance reporting unless telemetry and correlation are improved. [Data: Entities (243,241); Relationships (383)] ## Operational/process gap: PROD-only changes and environment parity hindered quicker identification Investigators initially focused on changes in PROD because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproducing across environments led to ADFS being initially considered but then effectively excluded at first. This indicates an operational blind spot: PROD-only changes can create cognitive bias when troubleshooting and reveal weaknesses in change-control or environment parity practices. context: REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction.

Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction.
